diagrams.title: Page Replacement Process Flow
diagrams.description: A step-by-step flowchart showing what happens when a page fault occurs and no free frames are available
elements: Page fault occurs when process references a page not in memory
Check if free frame available in frame table
If free frame exists: allocate directly
If no free frame: invoke page replacement algorithm
Page replacement algorithm selects a victim page
Check victim page dirty bit - if modified, write to disk
Read desired page from disk into freed frame
Update page table and frame table
Restart the interrupted instruction
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete sequence of events in page replacement and the role of the dirty bit in write-back operations

diagrams.title: FIFO Page Replacement Algorithm
diagrams.description: Visual demonstration of FIFO algorithm showing page frames and reference string progression
elements: Three frame boxes representing physical memory
Reference string displayed above: e.g., 7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3
Queue visualization showing insertion order of pages
Page fault marker (X) for each miss
Victim page highlighted in each step with arrow showing when it was loaded
Progressive steps showing each reference and state of frames
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize how FIFO evicts the oldest page first and understand the concept of replacement queue

diagrams.title: LRU Page Replacement Algorithm
diagrams.description: Stack-based visualization showing LRU page replacement with timestamp tracking
elements: Three frame boxes with timestamp/counter for each page
Reference string: e.g., 7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3
Highlight showing which page has oldest 'recently used' time
Counter updates after each page reference
Victim selection based on minimum timestamp value
Visual comparison showing LRU tracks usage history
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how LRU uses historical reference information and why it performs better than FIFO in many cases

diagrams.title: Clock (Second Chance) Algorithm
diagrams.description: Circular buffer visualization showing the clock hand and use bit manipulation
elements: Circular arrangement of frames (clock face)
Clock hand/pointer indicating current position
Use bit (reference bit) shown for each frame
Animation showing hand moving clockwise
Pages with use bit=1 get second chance (bit reset to 0)
Pages with use bit=0 are selected as victims
Circular arrow showing continuous rotation until victim found
diagrams.purpose: Students grasp the efficient implementation of LRU approximation using circular scanning and reference bits

diagrams.title: Belady's Anomaly Demonstration
diagrams.description: Comparison showing how increasing frames can increase page faults in FIFO
elements: Two configurations: 3 frames and 4 frames
Side-by-side frame states at each reference
Page fault counters for each configuration
Highlighting showing anomaly - 4 frames have MORE faults than 3
Annotation explaining why this happens (stack property violation)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand Belady's Anomaly, recognize that more frames doesn't always mean better performance, and learn about the stack property

charts.title: Algorithm Decision Tree for Page Replacement
charts.content: START → Page Fault → Check Free Frames → Yes: Allocate → No: Run Replacement Algorithm → Is Victim Dirty? → Yes: Write to Disk → Read New Page → Update Tables → Restart Instruction

charts.title: Page Replacement Algorithms Comparison
charts.content: FIFO: Simple queue-based, low overhead, can suffer from Belady's Anomaly | OPT: Theoretical optimal, requires future knowledge, used as benchmark | LRU: Good approximation of OPT, high overhead, no anomaly | Clock: Efficient LRU approximation, uses reference bits, moderate performance | LFU: Counts page references, good for frequently accessed pages | NFU: Aging-based LRU approximation, practical implementation

charts.title: Performance Metrics Summary
charts.content:
Algorithm | Complexity | Overhead | Page Fault Rate | Belady's Anomaly | Implementation
FIFO | O(n) | Lowest | High | Yes | Simple queue
OPT | O(n) | Very High | Lowest (theoretical) | No | Oracle required
LRU | O(n) with hardware | High | Very Low | No | Hardware counters
Clock | O(n) amortized | Low-Medium | Low-Medium | No | Reference bits + circular list
LFU | O(n log n) | High | Low | No | Frequency counters

branches: When physical memory is full and new page needed
FIFO: First In First Out - queue based
LRU: Least Recently Used - history based
Clock: Second Chance - reference bit method
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all page replacement concepts, algorithms, and their relationships for exam preparation and conceptual clarity
